---
title: "OGG... What's the difference between `git diff`, `git diff --cached`, and `git diff HEAD`?"
author:
   - name: "Ryo Nakagami"
     url: https://github.com/RyoNakagami
date: "2026-05-15"
date-modified: "2026-05-15"
categories: [git diff]
---

::: {.callout-note}
## TL;DR

:::{style="padding-left:1em;"}

```{mermaid}
sequenceDiagram
    participant HEAD as HEAD (latest commit)
    participant SA as Staging area
    participant WT as Working tree

    HEAD ->> SA: git diff --cached
    SA ->> WT: git diff
    HEAD -->> WT: git diff HEAD
```

`git diff HEAD` = `git diff` + `git diff --cached` combined.

:::
:::


## ðŸŽ¯ Goal

- [ ] Understand what each of `git diff` / `git diff --cached` / `git diff HEAD` compares
- [ ] Know which commands exclude untracked files


## Where each command compares

Git has three layers that hold changes.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    HEAD["HEAD (latest commit)"]
    SA["Staging area"]
    WT["Working tree"]

    HEAD -->|"git diff --cached"| SA
    SA -->|"git diff"| WT
```

:::{.no-border-top-table .pb-3}

| Command | From | To | Includes untracked |
|---|---|---|---|
| `git diff` | Staging area | Working tree | âœ— |
| `git diff --cached` | HEAD | Staging area | âœ— |
| `git diff HEAD` | HEAD | Working tree | âœ— |
| `git add -N <file>` + `git diff HEAD` | HEAD | Working tree | âœ“ |

:::

`git diff HEAD` is equivalent to the two commands combined.

```bash
git diff HEAD  # â‰ˆ  git diff --cached  +  git diff
```

However, none of them show **untracked files** â€” files that have no index entry yet.


## Walking through a concrete example

Consider this state:

```
HEAD:          a.py (committed)
Staging area:  a.py (first edit staged), b.py (newly added)
Working tree:  a.py (second edit, not yet staged), c.py (untracked new file)
```

**Setup:**

```bash
echo "original content" > a.py
git add a.py && git commit -m "initial commit"

echo "first modification" > a.py
echo "new b file" > b.py
git add a.py b.py          # stage first edit of a.py and b.py

echo "second modification" >> a.py   # second edit stays in working tree only
echo "c file untracked" > c.py       # untracked, never added
```



### `git diff` â€” staging area â†’ working tree

```bash
git diff
```

- Shows only `a.py`'s **second edit**
- No diff for `b.py` (not touched in the working tree)
- `c.py` is invisible (untracked)

**Output:**

```diff
diff --git a/a.py b/a.py
index 578ddd4..ab08efe 100644
--- a/a.py
+++ b/a.py
@@ -1 +1,2 @@
 first modification
+second modification
```



### `git diff --cached` â€” HEAD â†’ staging area

```bash
git diff --cached
```

- Shows `a.py`'s **first edit**
- Shows all of `b.py` (doesn't exist in HEAD, so the entire file is a diff)
- `c.py` is invisible (not staged)

**Output:**

```diff
diff --git a/a.py b/a.py
index 2fd0152..578ddd4 100644
--- a/a.py
+++ b/a.py
@@ -1 +1 @@
-original content
+first modification
diff --git a/b.py b/b.py
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..eaf0030
--- /dev/null
+++ b/b.py
@@ -0,0 +1 @@
+new b file
```



### `git diff HEAD` â€” HEAD â†’ working tree

```bash
git diff HEAD
```

- Shows **both edits to `a.py` combined**
- Shows all of `b.py`
- `c.py` is still **invisible** (untracked files are excluded)

**Output:**

```diff
diff --git a/a.py b/a.py
index 2fd0152..ab08efe 100644
--- a/a.py
+++ b/a.py
@@ -1 +1,2 @@
-original content
+first modification
+second modification
diff --git a/b.py b/b.py
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..eaf0030
--- /dev/null
+++ b/b.py
@@ -0,0 +1 @@
+new b file
```

---


### Diff coverage by command

:::{.no-border-top-table .pb-3}

| | `a.py` second edit | `b.py` | `c.py` |
|---|---|---|---|
| `git diff` | âœ“ | âœ— | âœ— |
| `git diff --cached` | âœ“ (first edit) | âœ“ | âœ— |
| `git diff HEAD` | âœ“ (both edits) | âœ“ | âœ— |
| `git add -N c.py` + `git diff HEAD` | âœ“ (both edits) | âœ“ | âœ“ |

:::


## Including untracked files in a diff

To include an untracked file in `git diff HEAD`, use `git add -N` (intent-to-add). This creates an index entry without staging the file's content.

```bash
git add -N c.py
git diff HEAD    # c.py now appears in the diff
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Code review workflow example

:::{style="padding-left:1em;"}

When you want an LLM to review all changes including new files:

```bash
# Register new files as intent-to-add
git add -N new-feature.py

# Pipe the full diff from HEAD to Claude
git --no-pager diff HEAD | claude -p "code-review for this diff"

# Stage for real once satisfied
git add new-feature.py
```

Without `--no-pager`, Git may launch a pager (`less`, etc.) which takes over the terminal instead of writing to stdout â€” breaking the pipe so Claude receives nothing.

:::
:::


## Summary

:::{.no-border-top-table}

| Goal | Command |
|---|---|
| Review staged changes | `git diff --cached` |
| Review unstaged changes | `git diff` |
| Review all changes from HEAD | `git diff HEAD` |
| Review all changes including untracked files | `git add -N <file>` â†’ `git diff HEAD` |

: {tbl-colwidths="[60,40]"}

:::


## Glossary

::: {.glossary-container}

```yml
- def: Staging area (index)
  description: |
    The intermediate layer between the working tree and a commit.
    `git add` writes file content into the index; `git commit` turns
    the index snapshot into a new commit object.

- def: HEAD
  description: |
    A pointer to the latest commit on the current branch.
    `git diff HEAD` shows all changes since the last commit.

```

:::


ðŸ“˜ References
---------

- [Git Documentation > git-diff](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-diff)
- [Git Documentation > git-add (`--intent-to-add`)](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-add#Documentation/git-add.txt--N)
